coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots produced by exfoliation of intercalated graphite nanoparticles and their application for temperature sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Duy Khiem
Kim, TaeYoung
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received much attention as a new class of fluorescent materials due to their unique transport phenomena and broadband absorption. Here, we present a method to produce pristine blue-luminescent GQDs from graphite nanoparticles by the intercalation of graphite nanoparticles and subsequent exfoliation in liquids. The as-synthesized GQDs are composed of highly crystalline carbon rings and show uniform size distribution ranging from 3 to 4nm with an average thickness of ∼1nm. The GQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 22.3%. Furthermore, the GQDs were explored as an active sensing material for temperature measurement. The GQD-based temperature sensors show high responsivity to temperature changes over the range 30–80°C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Temperature sensors
Graphite intercalation compounds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and properties of the GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Collaborative Research Program among Industry, Academia and Research Institutes through the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (MSIP) and Korea Industrial Technology Association (KOITA) ( KOITA-2014-4 ). This research was also supported by Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning ( NRF-2016M3A7B4027712 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) HR-TEM images and fringe patterns of GQDs. The HRTEM images indicate high crystallinity of GQDs with a lattice spacing of about 0.208nm. (c) Size distribution of GQDs obtained from TEM images (50 samples). The average size of GQDs is 3.5nm.

(a) AFM image of GQDs on a glass substrate. (b) High profile along the indicated line. c) Thickness distribution of GQDs obtained from AFM measurements (100 samples). The thickness distribution is mainly from 0.5 to 1.5nm (1–4 layers) and the average thickness is 1.03nm (3 layers).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs. The inset shows the photographs of aqueous GQDs solution under day light (left) and UV irradiation (365nm) (right). (b) Photoluminescence spectra of aqueous GQDs solution under various excitation wavelengths.

(a) Raman spectrum of GQDs. The small ID/IG value of 0.74 indicates high quality of the as-prepared GQDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of GQDs. Characteristic peaks related to the CH band (at 2930, and 2850cm−1), CC band (at 1640cm−1), OH band (at 1386cm−1), and CO band (at 1120cm−1). (c) XPS C1s spectrum of GQDs. The high C/O atomic ratio of 2.78 indicates the low oxygen functional groups of the carbon atom of the obtained GQDs.

(a) Schematic of the GQD temperature sensor. (b) I–V curves of the GQDs temperature sensor, measured over the temperature range of 30–80°C with intervals of 5°C. (c) Typical response current as a function of temperature. (d) Temperature response of the sensing device.
Quantum yield of GQDs using quinine sulphate as a reference.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots produced by exfoliation of intercalated graphite nanoparticles and their application for temperature sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Pusan National University (PNU), 30 Jangjeon-dong, Geumjeong-gu, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnamdaero Sujeong-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received much attention as a new class of fluorescent materials due to their unique transport phenomena and broadband absorption. Here, we present a method to produce pristine blue-luminescent GQDs from graphite nanoparticles by the intercalation of graphite nanoparticles and subsequent exfoliation in liquids. The as-synthesized GQDs are composed of highly crystalline carbon rings and show uniform size distribution ranging from 3 to 4nm with an average thickness of ∼1nm. The GQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 22.3%. Furthermore, the GQDs were explored as an active sensing material for temperature measurement. The GQD-based temperature sensors show high responsivity to temperature changes over the range 30–80°C.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are single- or few-layer graphene which typically has lateral size of less than 20nm [1]. GQDs exhibit size, shape, and edge-dependent electrical properties because the electronic transport is confined in all three spatial dimensions [2]. The complex interplay between the delocalized π-electrons (rendering high conductivity), size (quantum confinement effects), and edge states (providing either broad or localized electron distribution) governs the electrical and optical properties of GQDs [3]. Moreover, they exhibit stable photoluminescence (PL), high aqueous solubility, low-cytotoxicity, good bio compatibility, and superior resistance to photobleaching as compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [4]. Therefore, GQDs have great potential to be used in various applications such as cell imaging [5], biosensors [6], drug delivery [7], photodetectors [8], and humidity/pressure sensors [9], while few studies of employing GQDs for temperature sensing application has been reported [10].

Various methods have been used to prepare GQDs such as chemical vapor deposition [11], solution chemical method [12], hydrothermal route [13], micro fluidization [14], and electrochemical method [15]. Recently, the graphite intercalation compound (GIC) method was reported for the fabrication of high-quality GQDs [16]. This method is cost-effective and eco-friendly because it allows direct production of GQD suspension in aqueous phase without any surfactants or hazardous chemical solvents. The produced GQDs exhibit an average lateral size of ∼2.5nm and an average thickness of ∼1.1nm. Furthermore, the GQDs possess better crystallinity as compared to the GQDs produced from graphene oxide and show a photoluminescence with high quantum yield, which may enable various applications ranging from bio-imaging to optoelectronic devices.

Herein, we report a method to produce GQDs by the intercalation of graphite nanoparticles and subsequent exfoliation in water. Graphite nanoparticles (GNPs) were employed as a starting material and intercalated with potassium sodium tartrate to produce graphite intercalation compounds. Exfoliation of graphite intercalation compounds in water yielded homogeneous aqueous suspension of GQDs with uniform size and shape in a relatively short period of time. The produced GQDs showed a minimized amount of defects, suggesting an improved electrical performance as compared with GQDs prepared from graphene oxide. Subsequently, we tested the obtained GQDs as an active material for a temperature sensing device. The GQD-based temperature sensors are discussed in terms of their performances.

The pristine GQDs were prepared by intercalation of graphite nanoparticles and exfoliation of the graphite intercalation compound in water. First, 10wt% of GNPs (average diameter: 3–4nm, 93%, SkySpring Nanomaterials, USA) were mixed with potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (KNaC4H4O6·4H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and ground in mortar for 1h. Then, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and hydro thermally treated at 250°C for 10h to produce GICs. Following that, the GICs were exfoliated in DI water by sonication in ice bath for 2h. The resulting GQDs were separated by centrifugation at 13,000rpm for 30min. After removing the supernatant, the GQDs were re-dispersed in DI water and dialyzed with cellulose dialysis membranes (MWCO 6000–8000Da) for 5days to remove the remaining salts. Finally, the aqueous solution of GQDs was ultra-filtrated using a syringe filter with pore size of 20nm (Whatman, Anotop, Sigma-Aldrich). The quantum yield of synthesized GQDs samples was measured using fluorescence spectroscopy with quinine sulfate as a reference. The calculated formula was as follows:
ϕ x = ϕ s t .
(
I x
I s t
)
(
η 2
x
η 2
s t
)
(
A s t
A x
)
where ϕ is the quantum yield, І is the integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of solvents, Α is the UV absorption at 425nm, x is the GQDs, and st is the standard controls. The resultant GQDs solution was used for further characterization and fabrication of the temperature sensor.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPA-400, Seiko, Japan) was used to record the morphological images of GQDs using the tapping mode under ambient conditions. To prepare the specimens for AFM analysis, 10μL of the resultant GQDs suspensions was dropped on a SiO2 substrate and heated on a hot plate. UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs was measured with UV–vis spectrometer (Varian, Cary 50), and the excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer (Varian, Cary Eclipse). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30) operated at an accelerating voltage of 300kV was used to determine the shape and size of the GQDs. TEM specimens were prepared by drying a droplet of the aqueous GQDs solutions on a holey carbon grid. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Sigma Probe, AlKα) analysis was conducted. Raman spectrum was obtained from 1000 to 3000cm−1 using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR UV/Vis/NIR). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured from 700 to 3000cm−1 using an FT-IR spectrometer (FT-IR-4100 type A).

The GQD temperature sensors were fabricated on glass substrates. The source-drain electrodes (Ti/Au bilayer, 5nm/70nm) were formed on the substrate by e-beam evaporation. The electrodes were patterned using a shadow mask with their spacing of ∼50μm. To convert the hydrophobic surface to a hydrophilic surface, the glass substrate with source-drain electrodes was treated by oxygen plasma for 30s. The GQDs were then deposited by drop casting from the solution onto the plasma-treated substrate. Subsequently, the sample was dried at 100°C for 1h under nitrogen atmosphere to remove water and form uniform GQD networked films on the top of source-drain electrodes.

The preparation of GQDs involves intercalation of graphite nanoparticles using potassium sodium tartrate as an intercalant, according to the previous report with modification [16]. In this study, we used graphite nanoparticles (GNPs) with average diameters of 3–4nm as a raw material to produce GQDs. Because GNPs are much smaller and more uniform than graphite powders, the insertion of metal ions (e.g., Na+ and K+) between the layers of GNPs is facilitated and the reaction time for intercalation is significantly reduced. To prepare the intercalated GNPs, GNPs were first mixed and homogenized with potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (KNaC4H4O6·4H2O) in an agate mortar for 1h, followed by treating the mixture in an autoclave at 250°C for 10h. The intercalated GNPs were then exfoliated in water by sonication, separated by centrifugation at 13,000rpm, and re-dispersed in water, yielding homogeneous suspension of GQDs in water with uniform size and shape. The GQDs suspension was dialyzed with dialysis membranes (MWCO 6000–8000Da) for 5days to remove the remaining salts and filtered using a syringe filter with pore size of 20nm. Finally, a homogeneous suspension of GQDs was obtained.
The quantum yield of synthesized GQDs was measured as 22.3% (Table 1 ), which was higher than that of GQDs produced through solvothermal route (17.4%) [5].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and properties of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 1a–b and S1 present the HRTEM images of the GQDs. The diameters of the resultant GQDs ranges from 2 to 5nm (Fig. 1c), while the average diameter of the GQDs is 3.5nm with a narrow size distribution. These GQDs show a uniform and highly crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of ∼0.21nm corresponding to the hexagonal lattice plane spacing of graphene with d1100 [17]. This result is consistent with previous reports [16,18,19].

The AFM images shown in Figs. 2 and S2 illustrate the topographic morphology and the thickness distribution of the GQDs. The height profilein Fig. 2b shows that the average thickness of the GQDs is 1.09nm and the thickness distribution is in the range of 0.5–1.5nm. (Fig. 2c). Considering the theoretical spacing between two successive graphene layers of 0.34nm, the AFM result suggests that the produced GQDs consisted of 1–3 layers.

The synthesized GQDs dispersion appears light-brown under day light and shows bright blue PL under UV light (365nm) (Fig. 3 a, inset). Similar to the GQDs prepared by other methods, our synthesized GQDs efficiently absorb UV light (Fig. 3a). It is reported that the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains is responsible for the strong UV absorption below 300nm [20]. The UV absorption spectrum of the resultant GQDs exhibits two absorption bands at 250nm and 360nm corresponding σ–π and π–π* transitions from the carbine-like triplet state of the zig-zag edges of GQDs (Fig. 3a) [15]. The GQD suspension exhibited different PL intensity at different excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 3b. As the excitation wavelength varies from 300 to 460nm, the PL intensity decreased with increasing excitation wavelengths. The maximum emission intensity from GQDs was achieved at 420nm when excited at 300nm.

The GQD-based temperature sensor was fabricated using a resistor structure, and the schematic device structure is presented in Fig. 5 a. The device response to temperature change was studied by measuring the current-voltage (I–V) of devices over a temperature range of 30–80°C. The Fig. 5b shows the I–V curves of a device under various temperatures in the range of 30–80°C at intervals of 5°C. The current (I) extracted using a voltage (V) of 5V from the I–V curves in Fig. 5b are presented as a function of temperature in Fig. 5c. The results clearly showed that the current (I) increased as temperature increased, which can be explained by charge transport mechanisms inside the GQD network film. In a networked film of GQDs, the electronic conduction is attributed to the charge transport between adjacent GQDs by hopping transport mechanism and a tunneling conduction mechanism. At different temperatures, the conductance of the GQD network film is expected to be modulated due to the temperature-dependent variation in carrier hopping and tunneling at GQD junctions. Based on these results, the GQDs networked films were explored as a platform for a temperature sensor. The responsivity of the device is defined as ((R−R0)/R0)×100%, where R0 and R are the resistances at 30°C and the set temperature up to 80°C, respectively. The responsivity of the sensing device is presented in Fig. 5d. The temperature sensitivities of the GQD-based sensor extracted from the linear fit in Fig. 5d were about 0.62%/°C. These sensitivities were greater than temperature sensors based on gold or on PIN diodes [21], and also compared with those of other types of temperature sensors [22–24].

In summary, we produced a homogeneous GQD suspension from graphite nanoparticles by using the graphite intercalation compound method. This method is cost-effective and eco-friendly as it allows direct production of GQDs using water without any surfactant or chemical solvent. The potassium sodium tartrate used in the synthetic process not only plays a role in intercalation and exfoliation of GQDs but also serves as a solvent in the solvothermal reaction. The synthetic process time was significantly reduced to ∼14h, as compared to the previous report. The produced GQDs were homogeneous and exhibited circular shape with a diameter of 3.5nm and consisted of 1–3 layers, and showed blue PL under UV light (365nm). In addition, the electrical behavior of GQD-based devices demonstrated potential for sensing applications. Temperature sensors fabricated from the synthesized GQDs performed with sensitivity of about 0.62%/°C. The GQD-based temperature sensor showed a sensitivity greater than those of temperature sensors based on gold or PIN diodes or those of other previously reported temperature sensors. This report demonstrates that the GQDs synthesized using GICs are promising for use in highly sensitive electrical measurements and device applications.

The authors declare no conflict of interest. The founding sponsors had no role in the design of the study; in the collection, analysis, or interpretation of data; in the writing of the manuscript, and in the decision to publish the results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Collaborative Research Program among Industry, Academia and Research Institutes through the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (MSIP) and Korea Industrial Technology Association (KOITA) (KOITA-2014-4). This research was also supported by Nano Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (NRF-2016M3A7B4027712).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.09.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
